cuisine: Global spice; widely used in South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Southeast Asian cuisines

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of cardamom seeds is approximately 2 grams (about 1 teaspoon), used as a spice in cooking, baking, or brewing spiced beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Cardamom seeds, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for raw cardamom seeds (cr_id F10060010). All core macros and micronutrients are as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cardamom (NDB 02007) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spices and condiments — analogous estimate based on low-GI whole spice category; reference: Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002. Am J Clin Nutr. 2002;76(1):5–56.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cardamom seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on the general category of whole dried spices with high dietary fibre (28g/100g) and low available carbohydrate content. The high fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio strongly attenuates glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 2g serving = (32 × 0.13g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 0.04, rounded to 0.2 for practical reporting. This is consistent with the negligible glycaemic impact of spice-quantity consumption documented in Foster-Powell et al. 2002.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally low sodium (18 mg/100g) with very high potassium (1119 mg/100g), yielding a potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 62:1; supported by evidence-based antihypertensive properties of cardamom volatile compounds.
hypertension.guidance: Cardamom seeds are highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; use freely as a flavouring spice in place of salt, noting that typical culinary servings of 1–5g contribute negligible sodium while providing meaningful potassium and fibre.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high dietary fibre (28g/100g) slows carbohydrate absorption; low sugar content (4.4g/100g); bioactive compounds including 1,8-cineole and α-terpinyl acetate have demonstrated hypoglycaemic and insulin-sensitising effects in peer-reviewed studies.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cardamom seeds are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a culinary spice; the high fibre content and bioactive phytochemicals support glycaemic control, though per-serving quantities are small and cardamom should be used as a beneficial adjunct rather than a primary management strategy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.68g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (28g/100g), high potassium (1119mg/100g), and high calcium (383mg/100g); cardamom polyphenols and terpenoids have demonstrated cardioprotective, antioxidant, and anti-platelet aggregation properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cardamom seeds are suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; their negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high fibre profile support lipid management and vascular health when used as part of a balanced, low-sodium diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory volatile compounds (cineole, terpinene) and flavonoid polyphenols; high dietary fibre supports gut microbiome health relevant to immune modulation in RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cardamom seeds are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as an anti-inflammatory culinary spice; benefits per serving are modest but cumulative when used regularly in cooking.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cardamom seeds (Elettaria cardamomum) are the small, intensely aromatic black seeds extracted from the dried green pods of the cardamom plant, native to South India and Sri Lanka. They are one of the world's most prized and expensive spices, used extensively in South Asian, Middle Eastern, Scandinavian, and Southeast Asian cuisines. The seeds have a firm, angular texture and deliver a complex flavour profile combining warm, eucalyptus-like, slightly sweet, and mildly peppery notes derived from their high concentration of volatile oils including 1,8-cineole, α-terpinyl acetate, and linalool. In cooking, cardamom seeds are used whole in rice dishes, biryanis, curries, and spiced teas (chai), or ground into spice blends such as garam masala. They are also used in Scandinavian baking and Arabic coffee (qahwa). Nutritionally, cardamom seeds are remarkable for their exceptionally high dietary fibre content (28g/100g), very high potassium (1119mg/100g), significant calcium (383mg/100g), and high iron (13.97mg/100g), though typical culinary servings of 1–5g mean per-serving nutrient contributions are modest. The seeds are naturally low in sodium (18mg/100g) and contain no cholesterol. Clinically, cardamom bioactives have demonstrated hypoglycaemic, antihypertensive, anti-inflammatory, and cardioprotective properties in peer-reviewed research, making them a beneficial functional spice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The food is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: What are cardamom seeds good for?
Is cardamom good for blood pressure?
How much fibre is in cardamom?
What is the GI of cardamom?
Can I eat cardamom if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060010, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02007) for cardamom. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category framework and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: Saturated fat (0.68g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.87g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.43g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cardamom (NDB 02007) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these values are consistent with the total fat of 6.7g/100g reported by HPB. Trans_fat_g is 0.0g as cardamom is a raw plant seed with no industrial processing. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0mg — confirmed zero as cardamom is a plant-derived food; cholesterol is naturally zero in all plant foods. Fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond the core set, and moisture content, are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food; USDA SR Legacy reports some values but cross-source imputation was not applied to avoid introducing unverified precision. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as cardamom seeds are a spice, not a grain product. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cardamom seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Glycemic load per 2g serving is approximately 0.04 (negligible), reported as 0.2 for practical rounding. The input condition key 'diabetes' has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
